data.title: Franklin Managed Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: US equities collectively delivered solid gains during the fourth quarter of 2025, maintaining their upward trajectory against a backdrop of generally robust corporate earnings, despite some mixed economic signals and investor concerns about stretched technology valuations. Continued monetary easing by the US Federal Reserve (Fed), along with a constructive shift in US-China relations, supported investor sentiment.
  • Contributors: Allocations to both the fixed income and equity sides of the portfolio benefited the fund's absolute returns. On a relative basis, security selection, as well as underweight positioning within fixed income and an overweight allocation to equities, assisted performance.
  • Detractors: An overweight allocation to utilities stocks weakened relative equity returns, while selection among US Treasuries (USTs) detracted from relative fixed income results.
  • Outlook: We believe fixed income yields remain attractive, and we are constructive on equity markets despite volatility within certain sectors. Broad diversification and a high degree of flexibility underpin our approach to navigating the year ahead. Our diversified portfolio is designed to pursue income stability while selectively participating in secular growth themes using tools like equity-linked notes.

Performance Review

  • Franklin Managed Income Fund (FBFZX) (Advisor Class shares without sales charges) outperformed its benchmark , the Linked 60% Bloomberg US Aggregate Index + 40% MSCI USA High Dividend Yield Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025. It also outperformed the Bloomberg US Aggregate Index .
  • Over the course of the quarter, the fund decreased its fixed income exposure and increased its allocation to equities .
  • Fixed income allocations to securitized products, as well as the financials and utilities sectors, had a positive effect on absolute fund performance. In contrast, USTs detracted from returns during the period.
  • By individual issuer, Freddie Mac (FMCC), Fannie Mae (FNMA) and General Motors (GM) were the top performers within the fund's fixed income holdings. Meanwhile, Oracle (ORCL) hindered returns .
  • In terms of equities, the fund benefited from positions within the health care, materials and information technology sectors . However, the consumer discretionary sector detracted from absolute returns.
  • Albemarle (ALB), Merck & Co. (MRK) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) added value within the fund's equity holdings. Conversely, Home Depot (HD), Southern Co. (SO) and Texas Instruments (TXN) weakened returns during the period.

Outlook

  • Economy: The economic growth outlook continues to be a major area of focus for the fund. The US economy remains resilient, but ongoing uncertainty in monetary and fiscal policy, as well as trade and geopolitical risks, could weigh on investor sentiment and the broader economy. Moreover, while the labor market has incrementally cooled, unemployment levels are still low on a historical basis. We continue to monitor financial conditions as a leading indicator of future economic performance and Fed policy. We expect stimulative benefits from 2025 fiscal policy to support growth in early 2026.
  • Equities: Market breadth in equity markets has narrowed in recent months. While index level valuations are elevated, opportunities continue to evolve below the index levels, which we feel favors active management. Ultimately, given current valuations, we remain selective in engaging with equities and continue to focus on broad diversification across sectors while maintaining flexibility to capitalize on potential dislocations.
  • Treasuries/Government-Backed Bonds: Interest-rate volatility has declined, despite continued uncertainty regarding tariffs and concerns about their ultimate impact on growth, inflation and corporate fundamentals. Government securities continue to provide an attractive investment opportunity, in our view, as yields remain elevated based on recent history. We believe they continue to offer good diversification potential and can serve as a ballast to help hedge portfolios during market volatility.
  • Investment-Grade Corporate Bonds: Absolute yield levels remain attractive for an income-generating strategy, but credit spreads have contracted materially over the past year, which has decreased the attractiveness of investment-grade corporate bonds, in our assessment. With credit spreads at historically tight levels, we believe future returns may be driven by interest-rate moves and carry rather than further spread compression.

data.title: The Buckle: Strong Fundamentals, But Downgrade Warranted Because Of Macro Headwinds
data.description:
Earlier this year I wrote an article about The Buckle (BKE), calling it one of the few firms that I find attractive in the consumer discretionary sector. I praised the firm for its impressive revenue growth - despite the weak consumer confidence in the U.S. -, for its attractive profitability metrics and its strong liquidity position, while highlighting the relatively high valuation. Since then, I do not believe that BKE fundamentally changed. In the most recent quarter, the firm still delivered impressive results top and bottom line, beating analyst estimates. But looking forward, I am becoming somewhat concerned because of the geopolitical tension in the Middle East and its potential consequences on businesses worldwide. For this reason, I believe that my previously established buy rating is no longer warranted; a more cautious hold is more suitable right now.

Fundamentals and macro considerations

In the most recent quarter, BKE managed to beat analyst estimates on the top and bottom lines. Revenue grew by 5.3% year-over-year, totaling $399.14 million. It is not simply the sales growth that impresses, but that comparable sales were driving much of this growth. Comparable sales rose by 3.9% compared to the prior year period.

It is not only the most recent quarter that was strong, but the entire fiscal year 2025. Net sales grew by 6.6% YoY, from which comparable sales accounted for 5.6%. This growth was in general driven by a strong demand for BKE's products. The number of transactions increased by 4.2, and the average unit retail increased by 3.6%, partially offset by the 1.2% decrease in average units sold per transaction.

At the same time, the company also managed to improve its profitability metrics compared to the prior year. The firm's SG&A expenses decreased as a percentage of sales mainly because of three factors: non-recurring digital commerce investments made in 2024, store labor-related expenses, and e-commerce shipping expense.

Further, The Buckle also remains a reliable dividend payer, as they have been for more than two decades now. They recently declared a quarterly $0.35 dividend per share. This may not seem high, but the firm generally rewards its shareholders with special dividends - as their financials allow.

The firm's balance sheet also remained solid. Both the current and quick ratios are above 1. These liquidity ratios indicate that BKE's current assets are able to cover the current liabilities, which can be important during times of market distress.

For these reasons, I believe that BKE remains fundamentally solid. The demand for these products remains high, and the margins are also expanding, despite the poor consumer confidence levels. What I am not sure about right now is whether this growth and profitability improvement is sustainable in the coming quarters or not. Not because the firm is not doing its best, but because of the macroeconomic circumstances.

Since the start of the war in Iran, energy prices have skyrocketed. The Brent crude is well above $100 per barrel. High energy prices mean higher transportation and manufacturing costs. If a firm is not able to pass on the higher expenses to its customers, its profitability may deteriorate. In the recent results we saw that BKE managed to raise prices and still keep the demand high, but I strongly believe that the demand for BKE's products is elastic. For this reason, I am becoming more cautious, not only with The Buckle but with most firms that are in the consumer discretionary segment and where demand could be highly elastic. And this is why I believe that a buy rating is no longer warranted.

Valuation

When it comes to the valuation, my view has also not changed. I expressed in my previous writing that BKE's equity appears to be fully valued. And I still believe so. The firm is currently trading at significantly higher multiples than its own historic averages or the consumer discretionary sector median. On one hand, one may argue that it is because of the strong financial performance in the recent quarters. This may be true, but looking forward, I am not sure that this performance can be sustained. In my view, the macroeconomic risks are currently not fully reflected in the share price, and I believe the downside potential outweighs the potential upside.

We also cannot get much more optimistic when looking at the insider activity in the recent months. In March there was a significant amount of selling done by various individuals. I understand that there could be many reasons why one might sell their holdings, so this alone does not mean that nobody should invest in BKE. But normally insider buying is a strong signal that management has a positive view either on the valuation or on the future performance. In BKE's case, no buying took place, however.

Conclusions

The Buckle remains fundamentally strong. The demand for their products remained high despite the low consumer confidence in the United States. Net sales, comparable sales, number of transactions, and average unit retail all increased year-over-year. At the same time, the company's profitability in fiscal 2025 improved compared to fiscal 2024. The company's liquidity also remained strong.

The macroeconomic landscape has, however, significantly changed since my last writing - primarily because of the war in Iran. Since the start of the war, energy prices have skyrocketed, with Brent crude prices well above $100 per barrel. Looking forward, I believe this is likely to create headwinds not only for BKE's business but for most firms in the consumer discretionary sector that are not able to pass on the elevated energy prices to their customers.

In my view, BKE's stock remains fully valued, and the recent insider selling - or better, the lack of insider buying - also does not give much reason for optimism.

For these reasons, I am becoming more cautious and downgrading BKE's stock from buy to hold.

data.title: Kingdom Capital Advisors Q1 2026 Investor Letter
data.description:
Fellow Investors,

The first quarter of 2026 demonstrated strong performance despite volatility in the broader market driven by ongoing conflict in the Middle East. We feel fortunate that we avoided the AI-driven wipeout in software and watched multiple anticipated catalysts for special situation investments come to fruition. Kingdom Capital Advisors (KCA Value Composite) returned 8.01%(net of fees)in the first quarter, vs. 0.89% for the Russell 2000 TR,-4.33% for the S&P 500 TR, and -5.82% for the NASDAQ 100 TR.

Since inception in January 2022, we have compounded at 22.81% net annualized versus 4.80% for the Russell 2000, representing cumulative outperformance of over 115%. Returns vary by account due to rounding, account size, and timing of deposits or withdrawals.

Our top contributors this quarter were United Natural Foods (UNFI), Energous Corporation (WATT) and Alliance Entertainment (AENT). Our largest detractors were Magnera Corporation (MAGN), Mount Logan Capital, Inc. (MLCI) and WW International (WW).

Active Management

As discussed in prior letters, our special situation strategy can appear underwhelming in strong bull markets but tends to provide meaningful downside protection and uncorrelated returns during more volatile periods. The start of 2026 has reinforced this dynamic.

The first half of 2025 marked our most challenging period since inception, with the composite declining by over 10%. Since that trough, we have rebounded nearly 40% over the subsequent nine months. We appreciate your continued trust and patience through periods of volatility.

We were active during the quarter and exited several positions after achieving our targeted returns:

  • TSS, Inc. (TSSI): Following a ~50% decline due to delayed rack integration volumes (highlighted in our Q4 letter), the company’s results in March confirmed a successful facility ramp. The stock doubled from its lows, and we exited the position with gains, capping our third profitable cycle in this name over the past two years.

  • Energous (WATT): A historically awful consumer products business, WATT has pivoted to supply chain tracking solutions. We initiated a position when the company’s market capitalization approximated its cash balance, reflecting minimal expectations for the business. As the company secured meaningful contracts (including Walmart), the stock rerated significantly, rising from our ~$8 entry to over $20 at its peak. Following a dilutive capital raise by management, we exited the position with a return exceeding 100% over approximately two months.

  • SunOpta (STKL): After meeting management at the ICR Conference, we developed conviction in the company’s competitive positioning in shelf-stable plant-based milk and fruit snacks. We began building a position with the expectation it could become a core holding. However, the company agreed to be acquired for $6.50 per share shortly thereafter. While we would have preferred a larger position, the investment generated a return of nearly 50% in less than one month.

  • We also generated gains in shorter-duration positions, including Ziff Davis (ZD) (driven by a business divestiture) and Kodak (KODK) (supported by improving profitability and balance sheet strength). These situations reflect our belief that it is worth our time to pursue opportunities in less-followed areas of the market.

Core Positions

A notable addition this quarter is Alliance Entertainment (AENT), where we built a significant position following a post-earnings dislocation. Alliance distributes physical media (DVDs, vinyl, and CDs) to over 35,000 retail locations and fulfills online orders for major retailers. While traditional physical media consumption has declined, the category has evolved toward collectibles, supporting renewed growth in select segments.

Key elements of our thesis include:

  • Strong insider alignment, with over 90% ownership by insiders and employees

  • Estimated run-rate EBITDA of ~$60 million, implying ~6x EV/EBITDA with growth potential

  • Exclusive distribution agreement with Paramount, which has significantly boosted earnings in the past year. I am optimistic Paramount’s recent buyout offer for Warner Brothers (WBD) will result in Alliance securing an even larger catalogue, which could push EBITDA closer to $100m run-rate. They are also ramping an exclusive distribution agreement with Amazon/MGM beginning in January 2026.

  • Benefits from record-breaking Mach releases from Harry Styles, BTS, and Bruno Mars. Styles sold more Vinyl in the first week than any male artist since at least 1991.

  • Capital structure reminiscent of our former holding Abacus Global (ABX), which had significant capital locked up in their SPAC warrants. Alliance has over $110m of capital accessible if their stock trades above $11.50 between now and February 2028, which would turbocharge their ability to grow via acquisition in their niche, and put their balance sheet in a net cash position.

  • Elevated short interest and high borrow costs (50–75%), which we view as unsustainable over time

We continue to hold a large position in Net Lease Office Properties (NLOP) as the company monetizes its remaining suburban office assets. While the sale price of its largest asset (KBR) was below expectations, we believe the remaining portfolio still offers over 20% of remaining upside, with resolution likely by year-end. Notably, cumulative dividends received have already exceeded our initial cost basis.

We remain constructive on United Natural Foods (UNFI), following its December investor day. The stock has rebounded nearly 50% from recent lows, supported by improving sentiment and early signs of shareholder-friendly capital allocation, including share repurchases. The company continues to trade at an attractive free cash flow yield (>10%), with multiple levers for earnings growth, including margin expansion and declining interest expense.

Magnera (MAGN) has retraced gains following its late-2025 rally, with market concerns focused on rising commodity costs. However, management has reiterated guidance and emphasized hedging strategies that mitigate input cost risk. While near-term performance has been frustrating, our fundamental view remains unchanged. I spent over an hour walking through our thesis on the Value Cville podcast back in January. Finally, Enviri (NVRI) continues to progress toward the sale of its Clean Earth segment, which we expect will return a substantial portion of our invested capital in cash. We believe the remaining business will be well-positioned post-transaction and will provide additional updates in future letters.

Closing Thoughts

Our opportunity set remains broader than our available capital, and we expect to remain active in the current environment. We maintain a balanced portfolio of special situations and deep value investments, which we believe positions us well to generate attractive returns going forward. As always, thank you for your continued trust and partnership. Please do not hesitate to reach out with any questions.

Sincerely,

David Bastian

Chief Investment Officer

data.title: Gladstone Commercial: Trailing Peers In Net Lease
data.description:
This year, net lease has made a surprising resurgence as an attractive asset class. Following five years in the doldrums, long leased assets have once again reached the spotlight with some shimmer.

The trouble of rising interest rates caused a tumultuous period for net lease REITs. The credit real estate hybrid asset class quickly became one of the most volatile during the rising interest rate period as earnings began to come under pressure. A key theme of the last five years that has emerged has been the battle of the haves and the have-nots. In this case, the battle is over who has access to capital and can better manage their balance sheet.

The industry has seen a lot of changes take place during this time. One of the largest headlines has been the acquisition of Fundamental by Starwood Property Trust (STWD).

Also, amongst the changes has been a separation. For traditional net lease REITs, this period ended up being one of the most difficult, as each business was challenged individually.

This article will focus on Gladstone Commercial Corporation (GOOD) and follow up on my prior coverage. We will explore a simple but key reason that GOOD has underperformed peers.

Review of Prior Coverage

At the beginning of 2024, I published an article covering GOOD. The thesis behind the article was covering the company’s troubled portfolio and their shifts towards becoming an industrial net lease REIT. The article included an overview of the company and balance sheet, including their portfolio transformation.

Since then, the company has continued to push deeper into becoming an industrial net lease REIT.

We increased portfolio industrial concentration as a percentage of annualized straight line rent to 69% as of December 31, 2025, as compared to 63% as of December 31, 2024.

More of their office assets have seen disposition, and industrial continues to grow through new acquisitions.

However, pointing away from GOOD, the conclusion of the article was the recommendation to simply look towards a different REIT. At the time, I recommended Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT).

Over time, I have found the business model of GOOD to be problematic. Looking outside what I considered to be an inferior bucket of assets, there were other troubles around growth that were beginning to hamper the company.

As it turns out, time continues to expose a weakness in the business model of GOOD. The power of retained earnings in compound growth is too big to ignore.

Trouble In Paradise

The Gladstone businesses have always differentiated themselves through a value proposition of delivering capital back to shareholders through monthly distributions. While Realty Income (O) was known as the monthly dividend company, Gladstone Companies brought this model to their lineup of businesses of all shapes and sizes. Today, all four of their publicly traded businesses distribute monthly dividends.

This model of capital return has made the company stand out and become increasingly attractive to retail shareholders. Many of us are looking for monthly income to fund retirement or living expenses, and Gladstone’s dependability and yield are simply too good to ignore.

Historically, Gladstone’s policy across funds has been to redistribute most or all capital back to shareholders. For the business development company Gladstone Capital (GLAD), this looks like paying loan interest right through to shareholders. In the case of GOOD, this involves passing rent straight through to the shareholders.

If you take a quick look at GOOD against key sector peers, the difference in yield becomes immediately recognizable. Over time, GOOD has consistently had a higher share dividend yield than O, NNN REIT (NNN), or Agree Realty (ADC). The question becomes why?

If we are to reshape the math, we can observe that the company's earnings yield is not materially higher than these other businesses. Using adjusted funds from operations per share as the accepted surrogate to show the company's cash that’s available for distribution to shareholders, we know that GOOD has an earnings yield that is much closer to competitors than a company’s dividend yield.

For example, NNN's AFFO yield is 8.3%. Compare this to GOOD's AFFO yield of just over 10%, and we find that their earnings yield is much closer than the distribution yield.

This shows that what’s happening is actually a capital decision rather than a market valuation difference. With the earnings yields much closer together, it becomes clear that GOOD is making the decision to distribute more capital to shareholders rather than retain these earnings. The decision to distribute more capital back to shareholders is not unwise. It was not until the tides turned that the adverse effects associated with this decision became more apparent.

So why do some REITs choose to retain earnings in the first place?

For a company operating in the net lease sector, the benchmark is to distribute around 80% of adjusted funds from operations per share to shareholders. This leaves the remaining 20% available to retain inside the business.

Over the past five years, retained earnings have become much more important than just a buffer for the company's dividend payout. People usually point to a conservative payout ratio as providing breathing room for volatility in dividend payments. The net lease industry is steady enough and capital requirements are low enough that dividend sustainability should not be a problem from quarter to quarter for most REITs. Retained earnings have proven even more important for another reason.

An often overlooked piece of retained earnings is their importance to the growth of the REIT. More often than not, we assemble the capital puzzle of net lease REITs by showing their representative debt and equity split. When a company chooses to expand their portfolio, shares and debt are issued in a somewhat predetermined mix to fund the new acquisitions.

In the case of GOOD, the decision to distribute materially all of its earnings to shareholders meant that it had no additional capital left to fund new acquisitions during a time when external growth became increasingly important.

Rising interest rates have caused refinancing pain for net lease companies over the past five years. Earnings headwinds have driven shares lower. This combination is troublesome for companies that rely heavily on their cost of capital to be able to operate productively. The trouble has been uniform, as every net lease REIT has seen its cost of capital increase over the past five years as a result of rising interest rates and changes in valuation.

It turns out that some companies are managing this dynamic much better than others, as is almost always the case. In contrast to GOOD, which elected to continue to distribute almost all of that capital to shareholders and is now paying the price through muted growth, other peers have bucked this trend. I see one of the best examples as NNN.

Historically, NNN has elected to pay out a smaller portion of earnings, closer to 70%. This more conservative payout ratio, fueled the company's ability to use retained earnings as a key growth driver. Historically, this supported the steady eddy business of NNN, which holds the track record for the longest uninterrupted dividend growth streak of any publicly traded net lease REIT.

It’s not just the fact that the company has money left over from earnings to spend. Retained earnings function more as a “free” addition to the equity position of NNN, which means the company can also issue a modest debt at the appropriate mix to buy properties at what is known as “leverage neutral,” a phrase that has become increasingly important over the past five years.

NNN’s management team actually highlighted the importance of this on the most recent quarterly call.

Looking ahead, we expect to fund our 2026 strategy through a combination of approximately $210 million of retained free cash flow, roughly $130 million of planned dispositions, which together should result in manageable equity needs throughout the year while maintaining leverage neutral. NNN's self-funding business model can consistently deliver growth in good and challenging economic conditions. Our long-standing approach to capital deployment, remaining selective and opportunistic will not change.

In the case of NNN, this amounts to hundreds of millions to invest each year to grow earnings per share.

Back To GOOD

So, if we turn back to good, we can quickly see the problem at hand. By continuing to distribute essentially all of its earnings to shareholders through the monthly dividend, the company has no capital that it can reinvest. If we are to look at the company's current cost of equity, it’s simply too high to invest across the net lease market.

The company mentioned on their Q4 earnings call that it plans to continue funding new investments mostly through other capital sources like their at-the-market program and private placement debt.

With the availability via our increased line of credit, access to the private placement bond market, cash on hand and the ATM, we are positioned to deploy capital into accretive industrial acquisitions and portfolio improvements.

Capitalization rates of the big three asset classes are hundreds of basis points lower than the current yield of shares. The company lacks the retained earnings to make these acquisitions with cash, and leverage remains too high to borrow.

This, ladies and gentlemen, is the simple but powerful reason why GOOD has struggled to keep growing alongside a company like NNN or ADC. Maybe we can compare GOOD against STAG Industrial (STAG) given the industrial overlap, but the performance difference looks even more stark.

It appears as though the train may have left the station for GOOD as the competition pulls farther and farther ahead.

Conclusion

Sometimes it’s more than just the assets that make or break a real estate company. As the past five years have proven, balance sheet management is an extraordinarily important piece of running a publicly traded real estate investment trust. We knew that not all of these companies were created by people, but we did not necessarily understand how dramatic these ramifications would be. Companies like NNN that continue to operate a more conservative business model have proven themselves to be superior from a performance perspective compared to GOOD.

Different businesses often thrive in different times. There was a time when things went well without performing the market through higher leverage and higher-yielding assets. For some time, the trend was their friend. The case is now reversed, and when the tide went out, we found out that GOOD was swimming naked with a strategy that no longer accommodated growth in a rising rate world. In contrast to other members of the Gladstone family, GOOD earns a Hold rating.

data.title: Volatility Falls On Ceasefire Hopes, Yet Caution Remains
data.description:
By Mandy Xu

Cross-Asset Volatility: Implied volatilities fell across asset classes last week on optimism over an Iran ceasefire. Interest rate volatility declined the most, with the VIXTLT Index falling over 31 pts wk/wk to 85 bps vol as Powell signaled the Fed will take a “wait and see” approach to any energy-driven inflation increase. Both equity and credit volatilities came in significantly as US economic data surprised to the upside, with the VIX® Index down over 7 pts and the VIXIG Index down over 18 pts though both still remain elevated in the ~88th percentile high over the past year. Gold volatility fell 7 pts as prices rebounded – notably, however, investors used the rally to reset hedges. GLD 1M skew (25-delta ratio) jumped back to a 1-year high on the back of stronger put demand. Despite the risk-on tone in the market last week, oil prices continued their upward climb, with WTI crude ending the week over $111/bbl (+12% wk/wk) while oil 1M implied volatility held steady at 81%. There’s been very little change in positioning in the oil options market despite talks of a ceasefire, with demand for calls still outpacing puts and oil skew remaining at extremely inverted levels across the front 6-month tenors.

Equity Volatility: Options traders have been using SPX® options to fade big moves in the market in both directions. Last week we highlighted how investors have been selling out of hedges on pullbacks and switching into upside calls to play for a rebound. On the rally last week, we saw the opposite – demand for hedges picked up while calls were aggressively monetized (see Exhibit 2). The decline in SPX call skew and call convexity contributed over 2 pts to the 7 pt decline in the VIX Index last week. SPX 1M put skew (25-delta vs. 50-delta ratio) steepened off the lows on the back of stronger hedging demand, rising from the 2nd percentile low to the 37th percentile currently. Most of the hedging flow has been tactical in nature (e.g., concentrated in the shorter-dated tenors) while longer-dated portfolio hedging demand still remains muted. SPX 6M skew, for example, is in the 10th percentile low. In the relative value vol space, what stands out is the widening in the EEM-SPX vol spread on the back of the Iran-driven energy shock. EEM-SPX 1M implied vol spread recently hit a 10-year high of 16% (currently at 11.6%) while the realized spread jumped to an even more extreme high of 21% (highest since the 2008 GFC).

Term Structure: The SPX vol curve flipped from inverted to flat last week, with the decline in volatility mostly driven by the front end (see Exhibit 3). SPX 1Y ATM vol fell by just 1.7 pts to 21.0% (92nd percentile high). The 1Y-1M vol spread went from -3.5% to +0.3% (13th percentile low). While the SPX vol curve has flattened, the RTY curve still remains inverted (see pg 5 for details).

Cross-Asset Volatility Monitor

Asset Volatility Snapshot (10Y Lookback)

How to read this chart:

The chart shows how various asset class implied volatilities are trading relative to their own history, using a 10-year lookback.

The volatilities are ranked by z-scores, which compare the current volatility level for an asset versus its own 10-year history. A z-score of +2, for example, would mean that the current volatility level is trading 2 standard deviations above its long-term average (i.e., rich), while a z-score of -2 would mean it’s trading 2 standard deviations below average (i.e., cheap).

Because each asset class volatility trades at different levels and often defined differently (e.g., lognormal vs. normal vol, price vs. bps vol), by standardizing using z-scores, we’re able to compare different asset class volatilities on the same scale - and see at a glance if there are any divergences or dislocations in the cross-asset volatility markets.

data.title: Cardiff Oncology: Market Dismisses Onvansertib's Potential In First-Line Colorectal Cancer
data.description:
It is a common occurrence to see the market fail to react to positive data or updates from small-cap healthcare companies. Admittedly, most of these companies are still very far from approval and are often undercapitalized to get their programs through the FDA and onto the market. However, I still get baffled when I see a company post clinical data that suggests the drug/biologic/device/tech outperforms the standard of care in a huge market. Thankfully, some of these occurrences have allowed me to buy some shares at an apparent discount and eventually bank significant profit down the line as later-stage data confirms what was seen in the earlier studies. Well, Cardiff Oncology (CRDF) might turn out to be a great example after the market appeared to shrug off their recent Phase II data for onvansertib in first-line RAS-mutated metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC). Five months ago, I wrote an article where I pointed out this data release to be a big catalyst for Cardiff, but perhaps I'm missing something. For me, the onvansertib in this dataset and conference call discussion suggests it is a drug that has a legitimate pathway to becoming a first-line standard-of-care backbone. As a result, I'm looking to take advantage of the market's lack of response and will look to add during this period of market volatility.

I intend to review onvansertib's Phase II results and what the implications are for the drug, as well as the company. In addition, I discuss why I think the market reacted negatively to the data and the share price hasn't recovered. Then, I will point out some downside risks that investors need to consider when managing their CRDF position. Finally, I take a look at the daily chart to see if I can identify an opportunity to take advantage of the market's slip-up.

Phase II Results

The big headline for onvansertib's Phase II results was the 72% objective response rate (ORR), which is well above the 40%-45% range that is seen among standard-of-care regimens for that setting. Indeed, we can write a long list of oncology agents that produced early tumor shrinkage but ended up failing to hit the clinical benefit benchmarks in later trials. However, I think onvansertib might not join that list thanks to the durability of the responses. Cardiff reported that their median progression-free survival (mPFS) has not yet been reached and their hazard ratio is 0.38 for the 30 mg dose. So, onvansertib was able to show a response, and it is impacting disease progression in several patients.

I must stress that the control arm showed around 11 months for the PFS, so onvansertib's observed benefits look to be real and are not a result of a formulated attempt to boost numbers with trial design or patient selection. So, I would say the numbers suggest onvansertib's potential benefits could translate to bigger trials.

We also have to acknowledge the difference between the cohorts, the ORR increasing from 44.4% in the 20 mg arm to 72.2% in the 30 mg arm, with roughly 43% in the standard-of-care population. This difference tells me that we are seeing true biological activity that supports that onvansertib's mechanism is working as planned.

Last but not least, onvansertib's safety profile appears to be adequate to be used in first-line oncology, where tolerability is critical. So far, onvansertib has shown to have strong enough efficacy, with rates of grade 3 or higher adverse events remaining low, and neutropenia being the most commonly reported adverse event.

So, patients and providers could see onvansertib as a legitimate option for their first drug in their treatment.

Strengthening the Outlook

Although we are far from a potential approval, I believe the Phase II data gives investors enough to be excited about both the drug's and the company's outlook. In my previous Cardiff article, I discussed how onvansertib has substantial clinical prospects as a selective PLK1 inhibitor because it has the potential to disrupt cell cycle progression and DNA repair, which can be powerful mechanisms when paired with a chemotherapy agent that induces DNA damage. So, the combination should impair the cell's repair mechanisms, thus impeding the tumor's ability to grow or survive. Indeed, this is the same goal for countless oncology agents, but RAS-mutated cancers have been notoriously difficult to target directly. So, onvansertib could be a big win for RAS-mutated mCRC patients and their providers.

We also have to acknowledge that RAS-mutated mCRC accounts for about 40% of all colorectal cancer cases and had a total market of roughly $13B in 2024. Yet, the first-line standard of care has been mostly stagnant for decades. So, the RAS-mutated mCRC market is begging for a disruptive therapy to raise the bar, which would be a huge commercial opportunity... especially since onvansertib is mutation-agnostic within RAS.

The RAS-mutant mCRC market could be an enormous opening with ~153K annual CRC diagnoses in the U.S., with about 22%-25% being metastatic and roughly 27K-31K being RAS-mutant. Going back to my Cardiff Oncology article from May of 2025, I presumed that 70%-80% of U.S. patients will be allowed to have a systemic therapy; we are looking at 20K-25K patients per year for onvansertib. Targeted oncology therapies are often priced at $10K-$15K per month with treatments going out 6-8 months, so the U.S. market for onvansertib could be $1.2B-$2.5B per year in the RAS-mutant mCRC market. So, onvansertib's U.S. peak sales in the RAS-mutant mCRC market could range from $240M to $1.5B.

CRDF is currently trading in the $1.50 per share range and has a market cap of ~$108M, so onvansertib hitting blockbuster status would be metamorphic for the stock. Certainly, Cardiff is undoubtedly years away from getting onvansertib through the FDA and onto the market. Still, if the company was able to get onvansertib onto the market, the growth trajectory could be acute.

Looking at the Street's estimates above, it would appear that analysts are expecting Cardiff to start reporting substantial revenues in 2029, with some forecasting the company to climb above $1B in annual revenue for 2032. I must point out that previous estimates had the earliest timeline to $1B in revenue as 2034, so perhaps some analysts are starting to see the opportunity for onvansertib. Hitting $1B in revenue in 2032 would be roughly 0.10x forward price-to-sales. The industry's average price-to-sales is around 4x-5x, so a $5B valuation could be approximately $73 per share. CRDF is trading at around $1.50 per share, so I think it would be appropriate to say there is sizeable upside potential.

Undeniably, the company will have to continue to raise cash in the coming years, so the risk of dilution is high, and that $73 per share will probably come down. Still, investors could enjoy substantial upside even if Cardiff had to triple their share count from dilution.

Deciphering the Market's Reaction

I must admit I wasn't shocked by the market's reaction to what I believe is positive data. This wasn't the first time, nor will it be the last, that a small-cap healthcare company publicizes a positive catalyst and the market punishes the share price for a prolonged period of time.

Now, I must admit the market has some excuses to take CRDF lower. Despite strong signals, this was a small, randomized Phase II dose-finding study, so there is still a large degree of uncertainty. The 30 mg arm only had 18 patients, so the dataset is thin, and it wouldn't take much for the response rates to shift dramatically with only a few neutral or negative outcomes in progression-free survival or response rate. So, I don't blame investors for not going all in in reaction to the efficacy numbers.

Another likely concern was the fact that the company is not moving forward with FOLFOX for Phase III. So, we are expecting a FOLFIRI-only registrational trial, which will likely result in a narrower label potential but also the possibility of some additional scrutiny from the FDA. Obviously, the market would want that commercial flexibility to work with both backbones.

We also have to accept the data immaturity aspect, because the median PFS has not been reached. Again, this can be seen as something positive because there are low event counts, and it would suggest some durability. However, the market might be apprehensive until we see more mature data.

I will also point out that Cardiff finished Q2 with roughly $58M in cash and cash equivalents, which they expect will last into Q1 of 2027. So, the market might just be waiting for some clarity on the finances before bidding CRDF higher.

Yes, it is frustrating to see a ticker not get the bump you were expecting after a positive update, but we also have to accept that data is limited by a small sample size and early data. I would say there is a positive signal, and the biology appears to be real, so it might be a matter of the company getting onvansertib into a registrational-level trial before positive data translates to the share price.

Downside Risks to Consider

Despite my bullishness on CRDF, I must reiterate some downside risks that investors need to consider when managing their position. Clinical execution and capital management are going to remain my two leading risks. Cardiff needs onvansertib to continue to report strong data and maintain a seamless development path through the FDA. It is imperative that management ensure that their clinical trial structures are on point with the FDA and quick to transition to the next step. The company cannot afford to start up their next study and finish enrollment 12 months later. Cardiff finished Q2 with roughly $58M in cash and cash equivalents, which they expect will last into Q1 of 2027. So, the company is probably going to need cash in the coming months, and I don't see any major catalysts that could drive the share price higher before Q1 of next year. So, we could be looking at a ticker that is stuck in purgatory until they have reloaded their bank account, and their registrational trial is on the horizon.

I will also reiterate that Cardiff is basically onvansertib. So, the company is reliant on onvansertib to carry them for the foreseeable future. So, any setbacks or failures are going to decimate the share price. =

Despite these risks, I am maintaining my CRDF conviction level at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare "Bio Boom" speculative portfolio.

My Plan

At this stage, I view the current dislocation as an opportunity to begin building a position, recognizing that the risk profile is still elevated. I am not trying to time the exact bottom; instead, I focus on accumulating exposure when I believe the market is mispricing probability.

Admittedly, my CRDF strategy remains the same. I will continue to treat the market's mispricing as an accumulation window and will keep steadily building a position over the remainder of 2026. I have yet to institute any Buy Thresholds, Buy Levels, or Sell Levels, so I'm sticking with small additions on weak reversal setups, but will consider upsizing once we see some stronger technical ratings.

Looking at the CRDF daily chart, we can see the share price is trading below all anchored VWAPs, is bearish on the Go-No-Go indicator, and is weak on the RSI.

So, I'm going to wait for a break of the downtrend line from the January high before clicking the buy button. Then, I will likely wait for the share price to climb above the anchored VWAP from the March 2026 high before adding again.

data.title: Broadleaf Partners Growth Equity Portfolio Q1 2026 Review
data.description:
Performance Commentary

It was a rough first quarter for the Broadleaf Growth Equity Portfolio and the markets in general as investors tried to identify market leadership buffeted by AI spending concerns, talk of escalating private credit market risks, and ultimately, the emergence of War in Iran. While our numbers are generally in line with or better than those of the S&P 500 over most periods of time, the persistence of shifting leadership over the last five years has made this particular time period a difficult benchmark to beat.

Market Review and Outlook

The Broadleaf Growth Equity Portfolio has existed for eighty-two quarters and the S&P 500 much longer. Over this time, the BGEP has had down quarters twenty-two times and the S&P 500 twenty times. 25% of all quarters have experienced declines since we've been in business. Following these negative quarters, the markets have rebounded 70% of the time the very next quarter, often with a greater bounce than the decline.

When the markets have declined two or more quarters in a row – the other 30% of the time in the last twenty years – it's been clustered around more significant macro events. The housing crisis saw five negative quarters in a row, and the inflation scare of 2022, three quarters in a row. If we multiply the probability of a quarterly decline by consecutive declines, it would appear that in the last twenty years, the probability of more severe outcomes is 7.5%.

What does the pathway show negative calendar years? Both the BGEP and S&P 500 had negative years three times in the last twenty years, or fifteen percent of the time. After these down years, the markets bounced the following year, and often, more than they declined in the prior year.

To be fair, if you are down 50% in one year, you must be up 100% the next year to breakeven, so compounding is a factor. One of the three annual declines proved to be noise for both the BGEP and the S&P 500, but in the other two, investors would have broken even in 2-3 years, assuming you bought at the peak and made no further investments.

While the past two decades suggest a breakeven period in as little as three years, we always recommend a horizon of at least five years if you are going to invest in the BGEP or equities in general. One way to breakeven faster is to continue to dollar cost average into the weakness, buying the dips as your cash flow allows. This year is also a midterm election year, by the way, where the average intra-year decline has been 19%, compared to the average of 15% for all years.

Irrespective of the circumstances - and market declines all have their reasons - drawdowns happen. We should be careful not to minimize the current situation while also recognizing that the recoveries have been just as real. Living in the tension of two truths like this is a skill worth honing in our polarized world.

So, what were the contributors to the first quarter decline? We'd propose three. War in Iran, private credit, and AI investor fatigue. Last year also had its share of market swoons, brought about by tariffs, Liberation Day and changing immigration policies. Resilience has been a recurring theme in a politically volatile headline environment. In spite of those headlines last year, the market enjoyed its third straight year of double-digit gains!

With respect to the War, we've always viewed geopolitics as binary events that are risky when it comes to investment timing. Trump could exit this conflict as quickly as he entered it – reversing the recent declines as quickly as they occurred. We just don't know. In such fluid environments, it's even hard to put our thoughts into writing as before the ink dries the news flow could change, which is why this update has been a tad slower to publish than most.

One thing we do know is that Trump has shown a willingness to change his views, as evidenced by his TACO moniker, Trump Always Chickens Out. Whether this is a lack of courage or a willingness to change his mind with new circumstances we'll leave for the reader to decide. It appears he does listen to the markets – a balance of power that perhaps our founding forefathers hadn't considered.

Opening the Strait of Hormuz might be crucial to avoiding longer term economic effects for investors, particularly with respect to commodity inflation. But if the tariff turmoil of the past year suggests anything, it's that capitalists always find a way to bend and flex with the environment to supply valuable goods to those who are interested in buying. At some point, the valuable oil sitting in ships in the Strait will find its way to the markets that want it.

With respect to the private credit markets, a few higher profile blowups – loans funded outside the traditional banking system – have caused leaders like JP Morgan's Jamie Dimon to wonder if there are similar canaries in the coal mine. Since the Credit Cycle is indeed one of the key factors we believe influences near-term value in the markets, we aren't quick to dismiss such concerns. At the same time, we are not experts on private credit. We have had conversations with those who are, however, in some of our independent board activities. These experts believe these are isolated events and typical credit cycle dynamics rather than systemic in nature. Fortunately, most banking analysts don't believe troubles in private credit pose a risk of a run on the banks. As always, time will tell.

As a firm, we've long believed investors underestimate the value of liquidity in exchange for the absence of market-based pricing volatility -- i.e. non-correlated returns -- across the private markets. Growing redemption requests in the space may reflect this lack of understanding today.

The final contributor to weakness may be "AI fatigue", a term economist Ed Yardeni coined in the fourth quarter of 2025 to describe growing investor exhaustion within the space, particularly the Magnificent Seven cohort. This AI fatigue morphed into a Disruption Virus in the first quarter, with the technology decimating valuations across the software sector. Disruption concerns have also stressed some areas of the labor market, particularly for white collar workers in the tech space. At this point, the layoffs appear positioned as a way to fund capital expenditures promising future productivity.

Like geopolitics, AI fatigue and the Disruption Virus feel binary and may be subject to reversal. Hyperscalers are going all out in their commitments to further investment, while investors struggle with whether these investments will provide adequate returns. At the same time, while investors question the risks of this spending they have no problem bidding up valuations of those sectors benefiting from it. The wide swath of economic beneficiaries of such spending is immense and broad based -- across the energy, tech hardware, industrial and financial sectors. The influence of AI related spending is so immense that we're not quite sure GDP would be growing much at all in its absence.

On the one hand, if the spending by hyperscalers doesn't generate adequate returns, we aren't convinced they will keep spending. And if that's the case, then the sustainability of gains by the spending beneficiaries will be called into question, along with the Disruption Virus currently plaguing the software sector. On the other hand, if we witness an acceleration in revenue and earnings gains for the hyperscalers from their investments, then the Mag 7 AI fatigue may be unjustified. Can investors truly have it both ways? We're not so sure.

At this point, the spending is full steam ahead. When and if there will be a hiccup is apparently a concern for another day. We believe the fundamentals will remain strong for not only the spending beneficiaries, but ultimately those doing the spending. It is also conceivable that investors in private companies -- i.e. the LLM complex - are subsidizing losses that the ultimate beneficiaries of artificial technology -- the end users -- are not adequately paying for today. OpenAI's rationing of compute resources with activities like shutting down SORA may hint at this risk lurking beneath the surface of apparent good news of ever higher funding rounds.

After three consecutive years of double-digit returns, we've gone from an environment where everything has seemed to work in asset class land to one in which many areas are being called into question. Investors have learned that it is important to stay engaged with the markets in some way, shape or form, and at least so far, that appears to be what is happening, with rotational dynamics being more pronounced than the outright desire to raise cash. As we said earlier, seekers of alpha are scouring the market for hints of leadership in an environment constantly buffered by opposing clues.

Every morning, Pete and I go through our batting practice. And every day, we consider a potential trade or two. But in the face of so many binary paths – to war or not to war, to spend or not to spend, we lack the necessary conviction to put our thoughts into action.

So, we wait.

Historically, that isn't always a bad idea.

data.title: Pan American Silver: What To Expect In Extreme Market Volatility
data.description:
In my first-ever Seeking Alpha article on Pan American Silver (PAAS), I suggested its Q4 earnings report would be a blowout (see: Pan American Silver: Get Ready For A Potential Q4 Earnings Blowout). It was. But that wasn't a very difficult call to make given the company's strong fundamentals combined with the very bullish price movements of both gold and silver. Since then, and given the war on Iran and the extremely volatile price movements of both gold and silver (not to mention the stock price itself; see charts below), predicting the upcoming Q1 results will be considerably more difficult. But today, I'll give it a shot while also updating investors on recent (and quite bullish) corporate developments.

Recent Trading: Extreme Volatility

As you can see from the chart above, PAAS's Q4 earnings release was ill-timed to coincide almost exactly with a huge sell-off in the price of silver (as represented by the iShares Silver Trust (SLV)). As a result, PAAS's excellent earnings were overshadowed by the negative price action in silver, and as a result, PAAS stock sold off. The stock eventually recovered to hit a new all-time high just prior to the start of the war on Iran, which caused a "risk-off" run to the U.S. dollar and a further sell-off in both silver and gold. That sell-off beat PAAS stock down below $48 before recovering a bit last week.

But all that's in the past, so after a brief recap of Q4's results, we'll look forward to Q1 and beyond.

Quick Look Back at Q4 Earnings

The Q4 earnings report came in pretty much as I expected - although Q4 revenue of $1.18 billion (+44.8% yoy) only beat by $60 million, while I had estimated a beat of $100-$150 million was likely.

Diluted EPS came in at $1.07/share, which was +357% yoy as compared to the $0.30/share earned in Q4 of FY24. That was achieved despite a significant yoy increase in the diluted share count (to 422.150 million from 363.113 million, or +16.3%). A slide from the March Presentation shows how this remarkable growth in earnings was achieved:

As you can see, it's simply a matter of significantly stronger realized prices for silver and gold while the AISC ("all-in sustaining costs") for silver has been generally declining and the AISC for gold has remained relatively flat.

And, of course, it doesn't hurt that silver production keeps growing at a nice clip, although gold production was down yoy but +14,300 oz on a sequential basis:

  • Q4 silver production: 7.3 million oz (+21.7% yoy)
  • Q4 gold production: 197,800 oz (-8.5% yoy)

As I also predicted, the dividend was raised to the maximum per Pan American Silver's dividend policy (i.e., $0.18/share) because Q4 quarterly net cash ($554 million) was significantly above the minimum threshold as stated by the policy (net cash of $400 million or more) for a $0.18/share dividend. As I opined in my previous piece, Pan American needs to take a hard look at its existing dividend policy, considering the net-cash run rate is significantly higher than the highest threshold and dividend per its arguably outdated dividend policy.

PAAS ended FY25 with $1.215 billion of cash and cash equivalents - that's +40.8% from the $863 million at the end of FY24.

The bottom line: PAAS's Q4 results continue to demonstrate that the company can grow operating income much faster than costs. This extreme operating leverage is ultimately due to the fact that PAAS management has figured out how to very successfully fund growth projects and new investment initiatives without sacrificing margin.

Corporate Developments

On March 4th, Pan American announced its intention to resume buying up to 21,090,323 common shares (5% of shares outstanding) on the open markets - both the NYSE and TSX.

The next day, Pan American announced that exploration drilling in the southeastern and eastern Candelaria zones of the 100% owned world-class La Colorada mine in Zacatecas, Mexico, resulted in the discovery of at least four new high-grade veins and "associated contact-related replacement mineralization featuring wide mineralized intercepts with high silver, gold, and base metal grades."

Phase 1 development will focus on the Skarn deposit adjacent to the NC2 Vein. This is expected to result in higher-grade, lower-tonnage, and less capital-intensive mineral extraction as compared to the previously released 2024 Preliminary Economic Assessment ("PEA") report.

Christopher Emerson, Senior Vice President of Exploration and Geology, commented on the latest discoveries:

With silver assays exceeding 1,000 g/t in 40% of the reported drill holes, many with attendant high base-metal and gold grades, the discovered structures indicate the potential to add to the mineral resources at La Colorada, which we look forward to reporting in our mineral reserve and mineral resource update as at June 30, 2026. Importantly, these exploration results further substantiate our revised approach for a phased development plan at La Colorada that targets the higher grade zones of the vein mine and skarn deposit.

I am no geologist (electric engineer), but after glancing through the drilling reports attached to this news release, my guess is that the mineral reserve and resource update planned for June 30 will be bullish and a potentially market-moving event. So, interested investors should keep an eye out for that release.

Indeed, three weeks after the discovery press release, Pan American announced a revised PEA for the La Colorada Skarn Project, which the company says positions La Colorada to be a "Future Top-Tier Silver Mine." Subsequent to the announcement, PAAS held a webcast presentation on March 25 to discuss La Colorada, which included the following graphic:

As you can see from the graphic, starting in 2032, the production of silver and zinc from the Skarn Project is expected to rapidly and significantly ramp up and will extend for many years into the future. Michael Steinmann, President and CEO of Pan American, said:

The simultaneous development of newly identified high-grade veins at La Colorada along with high-grade portions of the Skarn deposit is expected to make La Colorada one of the largest and lowest cost silver mines globally, with silver production averaging 19.1 million ounces annually during the peak five years following construction and ramp-up. Further upside potential exists from newly identified high-grade silver veins not yet in the mineral resource and continued Skarn exploration that could lead to even higher production rates over a longer period into the future, demonstrating that La Colorada is a rare, world-class asset of exceptional scale and quality.

Note that the 5-year 19.1 million oz annual production estimate equates to more than 2x of PAAS's total Q4 silver production.

2026 Guidance

With the Q4 and FY25 earnings release, Pan American Silver gave the following guidance for FY26:

The numbers in red were added by the author and are (except for the *) the yoy percentage changes based on the midpoint of FY26 guidance. As for the *, note that in FY25 sustaining capital expenditures were actually a negative 284 million due to "the AISC reconciliation of payments for mineral properties, plant and equipment, and sustaining capital, inclusive of Pan American’s 44.0% interest in the Juanicipio mine and reduced for Pan American’s non-controlling 5.0% interest in the San Vicente mine." That quote was taken from the Q4 earnings report.

A few things jump out at me from the guidance. First off, silver production growth will be quite robust, while gold production will be rather flat. Secondly, AISC costs will be significantly higher in FY26 - and could go significantly higher still if the war on Iran doesn't end soon - see my recent Seeking Alpha article Phillips 66: Wartime Beneficiary of Surging Diesel & Chemicals Margin (Double-Upgrade) for my thoughts on that - while cap-ex is also going to be significantly higher yoy.

Q1 Earnings

PAAS is scheduled to release Q1 earnings after the market close on May 5th, with the conference call following at 9am EST on May 6th. The current consensus analyst estimates are as follows (courtesy of Yahoo Finance):

As you can see, analysts are expecting Q1 earnings of $1.12/share - a nickel more than the previous quarterly results (i.e., Q4 discussed above). That's interesting considering the chart below that shows that despite a wicked sell-off in silver during Q1, the average silver price in Q1 was still an estimated $25-30/oz higher than the average in Q4:

A similar argument can be made with the gold chart, with the average Q1 price being well above the average Q4 price:

As a result, my guess is that precious metals sentiment (rather than the actual silver and gold price charts) may be filtering into the analysts' estimates. Unless PAAS's silver and gold production is significantly back-end loaded (and to the best of my knowledge there has been no commentary from the company in that regard), and dependent on the timing and size of both share buybacks and silver and gold sales, I expect PAAS to post another significant earnings beat despite increased cost expectations. That's because, in my opinion, the higher silver and gold prices will swamp any increased fuel costs during Q1. Note the price of oil didn't jump until March, with relatively low fuel prices during the first two months of the quarter (Jan. and Feb.). As a result, my estimate is that PAAS will post EPS in a range of $1.25-$1.35. At the midpoint, that would be a ~15% beat.

Sentiment

Times have certainly changed since my first article on PAAS back in January. At that time, the price of gold was being driven by sustained Central Bank purchases, renewed investor interest amid a very volatile geopolitical environment, extreme U.S. policy uncertainty, and weakening confidence in the U.S. dollar as many countries - even traditional Democratic and NATO allies - began to realize they could no longer count on a President Trump-led United States when it came to the two things they care most about: trade and national security. Those same factors were similarly supportive of silver, which also continues to benefit from strong supply/demand fundamentals, as the silver market is expected to remain in a deficit for the sixth consecutive year in 2026 after an estimated deficit of 118 million oz in 2025:

However, the war on Iran has certainly turned around the drop in the value of the U.S. dollar:

As you can see from the chart, the US Dollar Index (DXY) has rallied ~4% from the low set right after the war began. That's likely not due to faith in U.S. leadership or that the U.S. federal debt will somehow decrease any time soon (indeed, in my opinion, both are significantly headed in the opposite direction ...), but more due to the fact that the U.S. is the largest producer of petroleum products on the planet and is therefore going to be much less negatively impacted by the impact of Iran shutting down the Strait of Hormuz (except, apparently, to toll-paying friends).

I think this too will pass. After the fallout of this war (again, read my recent Seeking Alpha article on Phillips 66 (PSX) referenced above to get my take on war scenarios...), my guess is that the remaining free Democratic & NATO countries are not going to want to buy the debt of a Trump-led United States that they see as increasingly aligned with Putin and Russia as compared to NATO and Ukraine (note that Russia is a primary beneficiary of the war on Iran). Along with the massive increase in federal debt due to the costs of this war to the United States, I firmly believe that silver and gold (as priced in U.S. dollars) will continue to be a very attractive place for Americans to invest going forward. And PAAS stock is one of the best and levered vehicles to ride the silver and gold investment themes. I also like Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) for similar reasons - see Wheaton Precious Metals: Streaming High-Margin Gold & Silver Profits.

Valuation

PAAS currently trades with a TTM P/E=21.8x. If we go by the consensus analysts estimates shown above for FY26 earnings of $4.41/share (which, again, I think is too low), Friday's close of $55.77 equates to a forward P/E of only 12.6x. That's a significant bargain in my opinion. Especially considering the company is cash-rich, has strong producing assets, and has a fairly clear line of sight (i.e., La Colorada) for very attractive growth potential moving forward.

Risks

At the end of the day, PAAS is directly exposed to the realized prices of gold and silver, either or both of which could drop during "risk off" market sell-offs.

Pan American's balance sheet is rock solid, ending FY25 with $1.32 billion in cash and cash equivalents and $873 million in total debt. That's a net-cash position of $446 million, or an estimated $1.06/share. Clearly, and despite investing in future growth projects, PAAS is capable of delivering significantly more dividend rewards for shareholders.

Summary & Conclusion

Based on the analysis above, I maintain my STRONG BUY rating on PAAS. That said, I advise investors to take a wait-and-see approach. By that I mean taking advantage of the extreme market volatility we have experienced of late (and which I suspect will continue over the next few months as the Iran war plays out and throughout the remainder of the Trump administration) and only making additional purchases on significant sell-offs during periods of extreme market weakness (which I also expect). If you have yet to take a position in PAAS but desire to do so, I recommend dollar-cost-averaging into a starter position (say 10-20% of your total planned allocation) and then adopting the previous advice about taking advantage of market volatility for subsequent purchases.

data.title: TQQQ: A Correction Could Be A Blessing In Disguise (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
In the very long run, defined here as a period of 10 years or more, I am bullish on the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) and its super-aggressive, leveraged ETF version, the ProShares UltraPro QQQ (TQQQ)—the subject of today's article. For this reason, I am selfishly hoping for a deeper correction in the equities market than what we have seen in the past five weeks, believing that this would increase the likelihood of oversized returns in the next several years if one deploys a sensible risk management strategy (something that, in my view, is a must-have when using leverage).

A bull hoping for a pullback may sound counterintuitive. Perhaps the obvious explanation is that corrections uncover the potential for more profitable buy-the-dip trades. While this is ultimately what lies at the center of my "bad is good" argument, today I am proposing a more structured way to think about sidestepping a potential correction and riding an eventual recovery in a more systematic but very simple way.

About TQQQ

Before diving any deeper, let's briefly talk about the ProShares UltraPro QQQ, a fund that seeks to produce three times the daily returns of the Nasdaq 100 index. It does so by not only holding the individual components of the benchmark but also by being long a series of index swap contracts (see holdings for more details).

On the minus side, TQQQ comes with a series of risks. The most important of them is that 3x leverage substantially increases the volatility and drawdown potential to levels that most investors would not be comfortable with—which is why, in my view, TQQQ is best held alongside cash and/or "anti-fragile" asset classes and instruments, such as VIX-linked (VIXY, VIXM), low-beta long-short (BTAL), and managed futures (CTA, KMLM) funds. The other key risk is volatility drag, which means that TQQQ could produce substantial losses over more than a day even if QQQ moves sideways or slightly up (find out more about volatility drag from the SEC).

On the plus side, TQQQ does not always have to be treated as a day-trading instrument. If blended properly with cash to reduce its risk profile, a portfolio that is invested one-third in TQQQ and two-thirds in cash can perform roughly in line with a simple QQQ holding over a multi-year period, as depicted in the chart below. This means that, yes, a leveraged ETF can be used as a tool by investors interested in a decade-plus investment horizon, provided that the tool is used properly (i.e., not as a gambling instrument).

With Risk Management, Bad is Good

Those who follow me know that I have written plenty about using moving averages (I know, technical analysis, yuck!) as a risk management tool that can increase risk-adjusted and even absolute returns over the long run. At a very high level, here is how it works, as I stated in one of my recent articles:

The idea is simple: own an asset when its short-term moving average is above its long-term moving average, and sell (or don’t own) the asset when the opposite is true.

As the Nasdaq 100 had corrected as much as 12% from the all-time highs right before the end of March, its 22-trading-day moving average dipped below the 200-day equivalent on April 1, as the chart below depicts. Based on the trading rule above, this would have been the time to sell the Nasdaq or its TQQQ cousin-on-steroids.

Notice that, in the ultra-short term, the decision seems to have been ill-timed, since the Nasdaq has returned about +2% in the first couple of trading sessions of April, and TQQQ has been up about +6%. But the focus here is on long-term trading diligence, not on making a quick buck before lunchtime.

A few things could happen next:

  • The Nasdaq 100 recovers fast, in V-shaped style, resulting in the moving-average trading approach forcing an investor to miss out on the first leg of the rebound but eventually getting the investor back on track to produce positive returns (a seemingly bad outcome relative to the benchmark, but certainly not a terrible one)
  • The Nasdaq 100 bounces up and down but moves generally sideways, possibly causing the dreaded "whipsaw effect" in which a trader buys and sells the Nasdaq a few times and treads water in the process (again, a bad outcome that is not necessarily terrible)
  • The Nasdaq 100 keeps declining and keeps searching for a bottom (relatively speaking, a great outcome for the investor using the risk management tactic described)

Allow me to focus on the last bullet point above, which is what I would shamelessly hope to see next. Below, I simulate what would happen to a $1,000 investment if:

  1. I held one-half TQQQ and one-half cash rebalanced daily for simplicity (one could work with the full 3x leverage factor, i.e., 100% allocation to TQQQ, but the position seems too speculative for my taste)
  2. I exited the position when the short-term moving average dipped below the long-term moving average
  3. My position produced returns that are in line with the price action from the following bearish periods, from the time that the moving average strategy triggered a sell until the Nasdaq reclaimed all-time highs again:
    • 2000-2003 dot-com crash (all-time high reclaimed only in 2015)
    • 2022 interest rate shock (all-time high reclaimed in late 2023)

The charts below depict how the strategy would have performed in both cases (the blue line is the moving-average approach, while the orange line is QQQ).

The first chart shows annual returns of 9% per year over the entire 15-year period, with only very brief moments of decline below the initial $1,000 capital deployed, against less than 2% annual gains in the Nasdaq 100 and sharp drawdowns below the initial investment levels. The second chart shows lavish annual returns of 26% per year over the shorter two-year period, against mid-single-digit returns in the Nasdaq 100.

Notice how I purposely left out the 2008 Great Financial Crisis and the 2020 COVID-19 bear. Both are classic cases of too many buy and sell signals being triggered, which would have resulted in the 1/2 TQQQ investment with the moving average strategy producing roughly the same returns as a simple QQQ buy-and-hold approach. This is evidence that aggressively owning a leveraged fund while managing the downside risk during bearish periods does not always produce superior returns in the longer term (i.e., don't always expect easy gains served on a platter).

Key Takeaways

I don't have a strong view on whether the Nasdaq 100 will recover from its (relatively mild) Iran War correction right away or whether we will see lower lows before the index eventually reclaims all-time highs. But using a moving-average approach applied to a leveraged ETF like TQQQ could prove to be a win-win strategy today.

Should the Nasdaq rebound quickly, an investor staying on the sidelines today could miss the bullish jolt in the next few days or a couple of weeks but would likely eventually catch up due to the leveraged exposure (assume stock prices go up over time, not down). Should the Nasdaq dip further instead, the same investor would likely produce market-beating returns in the immediate term (i.e., flattish vs. negative returns in QQQ) while being exposed to the possibility of buying the index at lower, more attractive valuations when the benchmark finally begins to recover.
